lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: That which hath made them drunk hath made me bold;

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: What hath quench’d them hath given me fire. Hark! Peace!

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: It was the owl that shriek’d, the fatal bellman,

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Which gives the stern’st good-night. He is about it:

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: The doors are open; and the surfeited grooms

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Do mock their charge with snores: I have drugg’d their possets,

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: That death and nature do contend about them,

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Whether they live or die.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Who’s there? what, ho!

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Alack, I am afraid they have awaked,

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: And ’tis not done. The attempt and not the deed

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Confounds us. Hark! I laid their daggers ready;

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: He could not miss ’em. Had he not resembled

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: My father as he slept, I had done’t.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: I have done the deed. Didst thou not hear a noise?

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: I heard the owl scream and the crickets cry.

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Did not you speak?

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: When?

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Now.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: As I descended?

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Ay.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Hark!

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Who lies i’ the second chamber?

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Donalbain.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: This is a sorry sight. Looking on his hands.

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: A foolish thought, to say a sorry sight.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: There’s one did laugh in’s sleep, and one cried “Murder!”

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: That they did wake each other: I stood and heard them:

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: But they did say their prayers, and address’d them

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Again to sleep.

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: There are two lodged together.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: One cried “God bless us!” and “Amen” the other;

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: As they had seen me with these hangman’s hands.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Listening their fear, I could not say “Amen,”

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: When they did say “God bless us!”

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Consider it not so deeply.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: But wherefore could not I pronounce “Amen”?

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: I had most need of blessing, and “Amen”

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Stuck in my throat.

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: These deeds must not be thought

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: After these ways; so, it will make us mad.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Methought I heard a voice cry “Sleep no more!

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Macbeth does murder sleep,” the innocent sleep,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Sleep that knits up the ravell’d sleave of care,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: The death of each day’s life, sore labour’s bath,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Balm of hurt minds, great nature’s second course,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Chief nourisher in life’s feast—

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: What do you mean?

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Still it cried “Sleep no more!” to all the house:

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: “Glamis hath murder’d sleep, and therefore Cawdor

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Shall sleep no more; Macbeth shall sleep no more.”

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Who was it that thus cried? Why, worthy thane,

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: You do unbend your noble strength, to think

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: So brainsickly of things. Go get some water,

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: And wash this filthy witness from your hand.

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Why did you bring these daggers from the place?

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: They must lie there: go carry them; and smear

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: The sleepy grooms with blood.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: I’ll go no more:

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: I am afraid to think what I have done;

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Look on’t again I dare not.

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Infirm of purpose!

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Give me the daggers: the sleeping and the dead

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Are but as pictures: ’tis the eye of childhood

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: That fears a painted devil. If he do bleed,

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: I’ll gild the faces of the grooms withal;

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: For it must seem their guilt.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Whence is that knocking?

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: How is’t with me, when every noise appals me?

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: What hands are here? ha! they pluck out mine eyes.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Will all great Neptune’s ocean wash this blood

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Clean from my hand? No, this my hand will rather

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: The multitudinous seas in incarnadine,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Making the green one red.

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: My hands are of your colour; but I shame

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: To wear a heart so white. I hear a knocking

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: At the south entry: retire we to our chamber:

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: A little water clears us of this deed:

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: How easy is it, then! Your constancy

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Hath left you unattended. Hark! more knocking.

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Get on your nightgown, lest occasion call us,

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: And show us to be watchers. Be not lost

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: So poorly in your thoughts.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: To know my deed, ’twere best not know myself.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Wake Duncan with thy knocking! I would thou couldst!

glosses.phrase: quench’d them hath given me fire
glosses.definition: the same drink that drugged the grooms has emboldened her
night-watchman whose bell rang at executions; here the owl cries as the bellman of death
the owl whose cry foretells death
attendants at Duncan's chamber
warm drinks of milk curdled with wine — here drugged
neglect their guard-duty by snoring
I would have done it myself
painful, regrettable sight — Macbeth's bloodied hands
the accusing voice; 'sleep' becomes one of the play's central image-systems, denied from Macbeth onward
the voice's indictment — Macbeth has killed not only Duncan but sleep itself, innocence, rest

glosses.phrase: knits up the ravell’d sleave of care
glosses.definition: mends the tangled threads of worry — sleep as a knitting figure
tangled skein of silk
the nightly washing-away of the day's toil
salve for troubled minds — sleep as medicinal ointment
sleep figured as the main dish of nature's feast
the most important sustenance in the banquet of life
the titles sum the man up: each of his ranks has committed a crime against rest
slacken your strong resolve — she is bracing him
weak in resolve — her most cutting insult
smear with gold — here, blood; a grim pun
must look like evidence of their crime
these hands will tear out my eyes when I see them
all the oceans of the sea-god Neptune
turn the innumerable seas blood-red; 'incarnadine' is to make flesh-colored or blood-red
to turn blood-red; to make flesh-colored (from Latin 'caro,' flesh)
turning the green sea to a single sheet of red

glosses.phrase: A little water clears us of this deed
glosses.definition: Lady Macbeth's blithe answer; the sleepwalking scene in 5.1 will refute it
I wish the knocking could wake him — Macbeth's first moment of open remorse

annotations.title: The murder offstage
annotations.body:
Shakespeare refuses to show the murder. Duncan is killed between the dagger soliloquy and Macbeth's return; what the audience sees is Lady Macbeth waiting in the court, listening for a cry, remarking that she would have killed Duncan herself 'had he not resembled / My father as he slept.' The one flicker of personal feeling she admits to all play.

The decision to keep the murder offstage is structural. *Macbeth* is never about the killing itself; it is about what killing does to the killer. The audience is placed where Lady Macbeth is — outside the door, hearing the act's edges, already complicit. When Macbeth returns with bloody hands, the scene we could not watch is inscribed on his body. The play will spend its remaining acts watching him try, and fail, to wash it off.

annotations.title: 'Macbeth does murder sleep'
annotations.body:
The voice Macbeth heard — 'Sleep no more! / Macbeth does murder sleep' — turns the act of regicide into the destruction of a category. Sleep, in the play's vocabulary, is 'the innocent sleep,' 'the death of each day's life,' 'great nature's second course, / Chief nourisher in life's feast.' To murder it is to murder the human's capacity for rest, forgiveness, repair.

The indictment is immediate and, for Macbeth, permanent. From this moment forward, neither he nor Lady Macbeth sleeps properly. 3.2 will show him begging for the 'peace' sleep brings to the grave's more fortunate inhabitants. 5.1 will show Lady Macbeth walking in her sleep, confessing aloud what she has stopped daring to say awake.

Notice the liturgical form. The voice accuses Macbeth by each of his titles in sequence — 'Glamis hath murdered sleep, and therefore Cawdor / Shall sleep no more, Macbeth shall sleep no more.' It is naming and indicting. The Catholic sacrament of confession, in which the sinner is named and his sin tallied, is the shape this voice is parodying. Macbeth's Amen stuck in his throat a moment earlier; this voice is the counter-Amen.

annotations.title: Blood and water: Lady Macbeth's first error
annotations.body:
Macbeth stares at his hands: 'Will all great Neptune's ocean wash this blood / Clean from my hand? No, this my hand will rather / The multitudinous seas incarnadine, / Making the green one red.' Lady Macbeth dismisses him: 'A little water clears us of this deed.' The exchange is the play's deepest disagreement.

She is wrong by 5.1 — 'All the perfumes of Arabia will not sweeten this little hand.' He is right from the start. The blood is not a stain to be removed but an ontological condition to be endured. The word 'incarnadine' — to turn flesh-colored, to dye red from the Latin 'caro,' flesh — is Shakespeare's coinage for a specific moment: Macbeth's imagination has moved from a hand to an ocean, and the ocean has taken on the property of the hand.

The scene's final dissonance — Lady Macbeth's confidence, Macbeth's dread, the knocking that won't stop — is a preview of the rest of the play. She will break slowly and silently; he will break loudly and on stage. Both begin to break here.

trials.prompt: Why does Shakespeare not stage the murder of Duncan?
options: The play is about what killing does to the killer, not the killing itself — the audience watches Lady Macbeth wait outside the door

trials.prompt: The voice Macbeth hears — 'Sleep no more!' — accuses him by:
options: Each of his titles in sequence (Glamis, Cawdor, Macbeth) — a liturgical form that parodies confession

trials.prompt: Macbeth and Lady Macbeth disagree about whether water can wash the blood off. Who is right?
options: Lady Macbeth — the stain is trivial
Macbeth — the 'multitudinous seas' cannot clean it; 5.1 will confirm his reading
Shakespeare's coinage from Latin 'caro' (flesh) — to turn blood-red

trials.prompt: Lady Macbeth says she would have killed Duncan herself 'had he not resembled / My father as he slept.' This reveals:
options: One flicker of personal feeling she admits to in the entire play
